topic: Locating Africa on the World Stage

study_notes.title: Mastering the Geographic Grid: Latitudes and Longitudes
study_notes.introduction: To accurately locate any place on the vast, spherical Earth, geographers use a network of intersecting imaginary lines drawn on maps and globes. This network is called the Geographic Grid System, consisting of latitudes and longitudes measured in degrees (°).

lines_of_latitude_parallels.definition: Imaginary lines drawn on a map running horizontally from East to West.
characteristics: They are also called 'parallels' because they run side-by-side and never meet.
They measure distance North or South of the Equator.
They are full circles that decrease in length as you move from the Equator towards the North and South Poles.
They are measured from 0° (at the Equator) to 90° North (North Pole) and 90° South (South Pole).
major_latitudes: The Equator (0°): The longest latitude. It divides the Earth perfectly into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.
Tropic of Cancer (23.5° N): The major latitude in the Northern Hemisphere. It crosses the Sahara Desert.
Tropic of Capricorn (23.5° S): The major latitude in the Southern Hemisphere. It crosses Southern Africa (e.g., Namibia, South Africa).
Arctic Circle (66.5° N) & Antarctic Circle (66.5° S): Found near the icy poles.
lines_of_latitude_parallels.geographical_significance: Latitudes help determine the CLIMATE of a place. Places near the Equator receive direct, concentrated sunlight (making them hot), while places far from the Equator (higher latitudes) receive slanted, spread-out sunlight (making them colder).

lines_of_longitude_meridians.definition: Imaginary lines drawn on a map running vertically from the North Pole to the South Pole.
characteristics: They measure distance East or West of the Prime Meridian.
They are NOT parallel. They are widest apart at the Equator and converge (meet) exactly at the poles.
They are all semi-circles of the exact same length.
They are measured from 0° to 180° East and 180° West.
major_longitudes: The Prime Meridian (0°): Also known as the Greenwich Meridian. It divides the Earth into the Eastern and Western Hemispheres. It is the starting point for world time.
The International Date Line (~180°): The line where the calendar day changes.
lines_of_longitude_meridians.geographical_significance: Longitudes are used to calculate TIME ZONES across the world because the Earth rotates from West to East, causing different longitudes to face the sun at different times.

the_grid_system_and_coordinates.definition: A grid reference (or coordinate) is the exact point on a map where a line of latitude intersects (crosses) a line of longitude.
the_grid_system_and_coordinates.rule_of_writing: Latitude is always read and written FIRST, followed by Longitude (e.g., Kampala is approximately 0° N, 32° E).
the_grid_system_and_coordinates.modern_technology: The Global Positioning System (GPS) used in smartphones and airplanes relies completely on this invisible grid of latitudes and longitudes to pinpoint exact locations.

africa_on_the_grid.a_unique_location: Africa is the most centrally located continent on the global grid.
key_facts: It is the ONLY continent crossed by all three major central latitudes: The Tropic of Cancer, the Equator, and the Tropic of Capricorn.
Because it is centered on the Equator, the northern and southern halves of Africa have mirrored (symmetrical) vegetation and climate zones.
The Prime Meridian (0° Longitude) passes directly through Accra, the capital city of Ghana in West Africa.
Africa is the ONLY continent with land in all four hemispheres (Northern, Southern, Eastern, and Western).
The exact point where the Equator (0°) and the Prime Meridian (0°) cross each other is located in the Atlantic Ocean (Gulf of Guinea) just off the western coast of Africa.
africa_on_the_grid.the_equator_in_east_africa: The Equator passes through Uganda, Kenya, and Somalia. It passes through Lake Victoria, but it does NOT pass through mainland Tanzania.
